data.title: What are the truly profitable sectors in the crypto payment industry?
data.description:
Everyone in the crypto payment industry is asking the same question:

"Which path can really make money?"

We've heard many industry stories, but very few have actually resulted in profitable implementations.

Today, I want to strip away the "mysticism" and focus on the reality:

Currently, there are only three truly profitable directions in crypto payments: payment gateways, compliance, and high-risk services. Other directions are basically just a matter of passion.

Understanding these three categories will give you a basic understanding of where the money flows in this industry, where the barriers to entry are, and how to get started. Channels: The eternal primary source of profit (and will become increasingly expensive) If you strip away the core business models of all crypto payment institutions, only one question remains: Who can deliver money to its destination the fastest, most reliably, and without causing trouble? Those who can do this can collect fees, profit from spreads, and build customer loyalty. (1) U ↔ Fiat Currency Deposits/Withdrawals This is the "vascular system" of the entire industry. All Web3 projects, game companies, cross-border trade, and e-commerce cannot bypass this. Why make money? High Demand High Frequency Costs Controllable Spreads + Fees Offer Room for Adjustment Fees ranging from 0.3% to 2% are all acceptable.

Future regulations will only become stricter, which means:

Channels will become increasingly expensive, and fewer and fewer people will be able to do it.

(2) Cross-border payments for enterprises (salaries, supply chains, global settlements)

A clear trend: Enterprises no longer want to distinguish between "fiat currency vs. stablecoins," they only wantusable, cheap, and fast. More and more companies will: Use USDT to pay freelancers worldwide; Use USDC to pay suppliers; Even use stablecoins for partial settlements. Companies don't care about blockchain technology; they only care about speed, stability, and compliance. Helping businesses handle cross-border payroll and settlement is itself a very profitable business. (3) Bank-level on-chain payments (tokenized deposits / stablecoin corporate settlements) Citibank, HSBC, and Standard Chartered have all issued their own on-chain "deposit tokens". This illustrates a reality: Bank-level cross-border payments are also starting to migrate to the blockchain. When you see banks issuing tokens, it's a signal that the industry is entering a mature stage. The profit margins for large-scale B2B cross-border payments are far more stable than those in the retail sector. This area is poised for explosive growth because it truly solves several long-standing problems associated with large-scale cross-border payments: slowness, high cost, and lack of transparency. Compliance: Seemingly abstract, but it's the true "moat" of crypto payments. This becomes increasingly apparent as you progress: you'll find that in the crypto payments industry, the most valuable asset isn't "technology," but rather "compliance capabilities." Many companies don't want to spend a year and a half applying for a license themselves, nor are they willing to bear the risk of rejection. Therefore, they choose to "rent" other companies' licenses, structures, KYC systems, and risk control systems. This is known as "license as a service." Commonly rentable compliance capabilities include: US MSB Hong Kong VA1 Token Brokerage Interface (Omnibus) Singapore MPI+DPT EU CASP Cayman VASP UAE Virtual Asset Light License

  • Hong Kong/MSO Fiat Currency Gateway (Does Not Touch U)

    • The demand logic is simple:Instead of spending a year applying for a license, it's better to get connected in a month.

    It's better to get connected in a month than to spend a year applying for a license.

    The profit structure of this type of service is stable in the long term: a fixed monthly fee + usage fees. Essentially, it's a subscription service that sells "compliance capabilities." Risk: Highest Profits, But Not Everyone Dares to Do It. Any service that banks don't like to do but the market desperately needs can be highly profitable. These types of businesses typically start with 3%–10% in fees, but you'll bear the full responsibility for KYC, risk control, chargebacks, and being reprimanded by the bank. For example: NFT and blockchain game project payments; computing power projects; cross-border e-commerce in certain countries; node staking/mining business; game top-ups. AI content monetization platforms often charge fees ranging from 3% to 10%. The reason is simple: conventional institutions dare not do it, and those who do can charge a premium. However—the risk control and compliance pressures of this type of business are extremely high, and those without experience are prone to failure. The Three Most Certain Growth Sectors in the Next Three Years I've summarized the most obvious trends in the industry into three sentences: 1. Enterprise-grade stablecoin cross-border payments will become the next trillion-dollar market. USDC/EURC/tokenized deposits have seen explosive growth this year. Citibank, Standard Chartered, and HSBC have already announced their entry into the market. Whoever can help businesses successfully implement stablecoin cross-border settlements will grab the biggest slice of the pie over the next three years. 2. Crypto Payroll: From the Margins to the Mainstream Traditional payroll methods struggle with cross-border and stablecoin settlements. Web3 projects and AI companies are recruiting globally, making crypto payroll a routine system requirement. This will ultimately lead to a "crypto payroll version of Deel." 3. The combined service of licenses, structure, and interfaces will continue to be strong. The old model of "each finding their own supplier" is disappearing. Now, companies are more willing to directly purchase: structural design, compliance system, KYC/AML, a complete set of documents, and a working interface (MSB, VA1, MPI, CASP, etc.). Companies are willing to pay for this "from design to operation" overall solution. This is a long-term, stable, and continuously growing trend. How should business owners get involved? In short: Choose the category you can do; don't try to be in all three.

    • If you have channel resources → become a channel provider

    • If you have local physical entities and risk control capabilities → sell compliance

    • If you have scenarios and merchants → do high-risk payments

    • If you have nothing → start by "helping enterprises run stablecoin payments"

    This industry doesn't rely on gimmicks to make money, but on solid execution capabilities.

    Making money in this industry doesn't rely on gimmicks, but on solid execution capabilities.

    If you're ready to get into crypto payments, what services can I offer? Many bosses who see this will ask: "So, if I'm doing encrypted payments, what specific help can you provide?" To summarize, the core services we've provided in Web3, payments, U-cards, and stablecoin projects over the years are all "practical and usable": (1) Helping you design a "regulatory acceptable" business model. Can your business be done? style="text-align: left;">Which links cannot be touched

  • How to design to avoid crossing the red line

  • How to get banks to accept your structure

  • How to connect with securities firms, PSP, VATP

    • (2) Help you build a cross-border structure (Hong Kong / Singapore / BVI / Cayman / EU)

    • Should I apply for a license? What license should I apply for?

      • Should I apply for a license? What license should I apply for?

        • Should I apply for a license?

        • Cooperation Agreement for Card Issuance/Acquiring/Channel

        • Complete Set of Documents Required for Bank/Securities Firm/Platform Integration

        These are crucial for the smooth operation of a business.

      These are key to the smooth operation of a business.

    (4) Helping you truly connect with banks, securities firms, VATP, and conduits. This isn't just conceptual, but practical: How does MSB open a bank account? How does Hong Kong MSO receive funds? Can VA1 open an omnibus mode for you? How do you use the proper route for stablecoin deposits and withdrawals? How to achieve compliant implementation of USDC → fiat currency? (5) Help you design stablecoin payment/on-chain salary solutions. Includes: How to pay global employees with USDC. How to ensure enterprises do not cross the line when using stablecoin settlement. How to integrate on-chain payments into your actual business scenarios. In short: If you want to implement a payment system, I can help you streamline the entire process from "business design → compliance structure → documentation system → bank channels" until it's truly ready for launch and operation.

data.title: Why is the Agent Oracle the foundation of AI×Web3?
data.description:
Author: 0xhhh Source: X, @hhhx402

Over the past few months, while working on the Agent system, I've become increasingly aware of something that's been severely underestimated: no matter how strong LLM becomes, it can't reliably determine the state of the real world. Once the Agent enters the actual execution layer—opening an account, trading, accessing a website, submitting forms—it's exposed to extremely high vulnerabilities. Because it lacks a "reality layer." What we lack is the Agent Oracle, which is practically the cornerstone of the entire Agent ecosystem, yet has long been ignored.

Why is LLM insufficient? Because the essence of LLM's capability is to generate text with the optimal probability, not to infer the truth of the world. It can't verify the authenticity of news, identify phishing links, determine whether an API is contaminated, understand whether a regulation is truly in effect, or accurately grasp the true bias behind Powell's speech.

These all fall under the category of "verification," not "prediction." Therefore, LLM itself can never be the "source of truth" for the Agent. Traditional Oracles are even less capable of solving this problem. They excel at price truth: structured, quantifiable, and observable data such as ETH/USD, BTC/BNB, indices, forex, and on-chain TVL. But Agents face a completely different reality: unstructured events, multi-source conflicts, semantic judgments, real-time changes, and blurred boundaries—this is event truth, an order of magnitude more complex than price truth. Event truth ≠ price truth; their mechanisms are entirely different. Sora's proposed event verification market is currently the closest attempt to the right direction. Sora's core shift is that truth is no longer generated by node voting, but by Agents performing real verification tasks. A query involves data scraping (TLS, Hash, IPFS), outlier filtering (MAD), LLM semantic verification, multi-agent reputation-weighted aggregation, reputation updates, and challenge penalties. Sora's key insight is Earn = Reputation: revenue comes from reputation, and reputation comes from long-term, genuine work, not stake or self-declaration. This direction is revolutionary, but it's still not open enough—real-world event verification experts are extremely diverse, ranging from finance, regulation, healthcare, and multilingualism to security auditing, fraud detection, on-chain monitoring, and industry experience. No single team can build an agent cluster covering all these areas. Therefore, what we need is an open, multi-participant "truth-seeking market." Why? Because the way humans obtain truth is not by asking a single expert, but by checking multiple sources, asking multiple friends, listening to multiple KOLs, and then extracting a stable understanding from the conflicts. The agent world must also evolve along this mechanism. We are currently building a combination of ERC8004 and x402. ERC8004 is responsible for establishing a programmable reputation layer, recording each agent's historical performance, call count, success stories, challenge records, area of ​​expertise, stability, etc., allowing a "verifiable career" to naturally determine an agent's eligibility to participate. x402 is responsible for the payment layer, through which we can dynamically convene multiple agents with medium to high reputations in a single event verification, allowing them to verify in parallel, cross-validate, and aggregate the results based on their contributions. It's not about finding an expert, but about assembling a committee—this is the "truth committee" of the machine world. An open, multi-entity, reputation-weighted, challenge-incentivized, and automatically evolving truth market may be the true future form of Oracle. Meanwhile, Intuition is building another layer: Semantic Truth. Not all truths can be obtained through event verification, such as "whether a project is trustworthy," "whether the governance quality is good," "whether the community likes a product," "whether a developer is reliable," and "whether a viewpoint is accepted by the mainstream." These aren't yes/no statements, but rather social consensus, best expressed using TRUST's triples (Atom — Predicate — Object), with consensus strength accumulated through stakes for or against. It's suitable for long-term facts like reputation, preferences, risk levels, and labels. However, their current product experience is indeed terrible. For example, to create the statement "V God is the founder of Ethereum," all related terms must have an identity within the system, making the process very awkward. The pain points are clear, but their solution isn't good enough yet. Therefore, the future truth structure will present two complementary layers: event truth (Agent Oracle) responsible for the real-time world, and semantic truth (TRUST) responsible for long-term consensus, together forming the truth foundation of AI. The Reality Stack will be clearly divided into three layers: the event truth layer (Sora / ERC8004 + x402), the semantic truth layer (TRUST), and the final settlement layer (L1/L2 blockchain). This structure is likely to become the true foundation of AI × Web3. Why will this change the entire internet? Because today's agents cannot verify authenticity, determine origin, avoid fraud, prevent data contamination, undertake high-risk actions, or cross-check like humans. Without Agent Oracles, the Agent economy cannot exist; but with them, for the first time, we can build a verifiable reality layer for AI. Agent Oracle = the reality foundation of AI. The Oracles of the future will not be a network of nodes, but composed of countless professional agents: they accumulate reputation through income, participate in verification through reputation, obtain new jobs and challenges through verification, automatically collaborate, automatically divide labor, and self-evolve, ultimately expanding to all knowledge domains. That will be a true machine society truth market. Blockchain gives us a trusted ledger, but the Agent era needs trusted reality, trusted events, trusted semantics, trusted judgment, and trusted execution. Without Agent Oracles, AI cannot operate safely in the world; with them, we can build a "reality layer" for machines for the first time. The future belongs to protocols that can help machines understand the real world.

data.title: Saudi Arabia's increased investment in quantum computing: Is this a new threat to Bitcoin?
data.description: Saudi Aramco stated that the 200-qubit computer, manufactured by the French neutral atom quantum computing company Pasqal, has been installed at its own Dharan data center, which is specifically designed for industrial applications such as energy modeling and materials research. Quantum computing appears to be progressing faster than previously thought. In a blog post on November 13th, quantum computing researcher Scott Aaronson (Schlumberger Centennial Professor of Computer Science at the University of Texas at Austin) wrote, "Given the astonishing pace of current hardware development," in 2028... Before the 2018 US presidential election, building a fault-tolerant quantum computer capable of running Shor's algorithm was "very likely." However, the question remains: is this a serious threat or a blind attempt? Auh stated that national-level investment motivations extend beyond cryptanalysis. According to research scientist Ian McCormack, 200-qubit systems are small in scale for practical applications because current machines are limited by noise and short coherence times, resulting in a limited number of operations that can be performed. "200 qubits are enough for some interesting experiments and demonstrations—provided the qubits are of high enough quality, but even such a small number is difficult to achieve. However, this is far from sufficient for error correction calculations, which is precisely the kind of computational power needed to execute Shor's Algorithm." He explained that this quantum algorithm is used to solve for the prime factorization of integers. Eli Bataille, a graduate student at Caltech, stated, "What you need is a very long coherence time, far longer than the duration of your operation. If your operation duration is one microsecond and your coherence time is one second, that means you can perform approximately one million operations." Worryingly, this capability would not only undermine the cryptography used by Bitcoin but also many of the security systems underpinning the global economy. "Quantum computing has a considerable probability (more than 5%) of posing a significant, even life-or-death, long-term risk to Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies," mentioned Christopher Peckter, professor of computer science and engineering at the University of Michigan. "But in the next few years, this is unlikely to pose a real risk; quantum computing technology is still a long way from threatening modern cryptography." More importantly, based on market reaction, this decline may continue. Funds are continuously flowing out. Looking at ETFs, in November, Bitcoin exchange-traded funds (ETFs) saw outflows of $3.5 billion, the largest single-day outflow since February. “This indicates that institutional investors have stopped allocating to Bitcoin,” said Marcus Tillen, founder and CEO of 10X Research. “ETF institutions have turned to selling, and as long as they continue to sell, I think it will be difficult for the market to sustain an upward trend or rebound.” Of course, there is some positive news; the Federal Reserve's monetary policy is showing a more dovish stance. According to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in December is 84.9%, while the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 15.1%. The probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by January next year is 66.4%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 11.1%, and the probability of cutting interest rates by a cumulative 50 basis points is 22.6%. Coindesk analyst Omkar Godbole stated that Bitcoin's first resistance level to watch is the 200-hour Simple Moving Average (SMA), currently near $88,000. This level has been acting as resistance for upward price movement since Monday, limiting gains. The next resistance level to watch is in the $98,000–$99,000 range, an area that previously formed intraday lows earlier this month and in June. Even Arthur Hayes, usually a bullish analyst, has unusually predicted that Bitcoin's price will remain below $90,000 and may retest the effective support level of $80,000. More pessimistic crypto analyst @ali_charts even suggested key support levels at $75,740, $56,160, and $52,820. Of course, optimistic institutions still exist, but their focus is more on ETH. Liquid Capital founder Yi Lihua stated on social media today: "Based on investment research data, ETH has been subject to large-scale short selling by multiple platforms and institutions. I believe that after weathering the most difficult November, a short squeeze may follow. Compared to ETH four years ago, the stablecoin/ETF/DAT/policy environment is completely different, and ETH is now in a completely favorable policy environment." The price is severely undervalued.

data.title: The new round of accusations has once again thrust Changpeng Zhao into the eye of the global storm.
data.description: The following is all the publicly known information about this situation. The latest claim originates from a post by DefiWimar on X, which cites a recent Financial Times report. According to the post, Changpeng Zhao is now accused of "helping to funnel funds to Hamas," a statement that immediately raised concerns about another market shock. The post includes an image of the Financial Times article and a direct link confirming a new civil lawsuit has been filed in a U.S. federal court. According to the Financial Times, the plaintiffs are families of victims of the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023. They accuse Changpeng Zhao and Binance of facilitating payments to support the attack and are seeking damages under the Anti-Terrorism Act. Importantly, this is a civil lawsuit, not a new criminal charge. Civil lawsuits have a lower burden of proof, focus on damages, and are independent of any presidential pardon. This distinction is crucial because Changpeng Zhao received a pardon from President Donald Trump on October 23, 2025, for his previous federal conviction for violating anti-money laundering regulations. However, a pardon only absolves him of criminal penalties and does not prevent privately filed civil lawsuits. The context of this case is critical. This is not the first time Binance and Changpeng Zhao have faced allegations of facilitating the flow of funds related to sanctioned or terrorist organizations. In November 2023, Binance and Changpeng Zhao pleaded guilty to multiple major federal charges in the United States. At the time, prosecutors and regulators stated that Binance violated anti-money laundering regulations, operated without a proper license, and processed transactions involving Iran, ISIS, Hamas, and other high-risk entities. The settlement, totaling $4.3 billion, was one of the largest fines in U.S. history for such violations. As part of the plea agreement, Changpeng Zhao resigned as CEO and began serving a four-month federal prison sentence in April 2024. In February 2024, another civil lawsuit against Binance (initially not directly against Changpeng Zhao) was filed by the families of the victims and hostages of the October 7 attacks. The lawsuit accuses Binance of acting as a source of funds for Hamas between 2017 and 2023 due to its failure to implement proper controls and allowing transactions that should have been flagged or blocked. This earlier lawsuit forms part of a broader pattern that has resurfaced in this new lawsuit. However, this new lawsuit directly targets Changpeng Zhao, and comes after his return to the public eye following a pardon. The lawsuit claims that Binance's long-standing compliance issues, coupled with Zhao's personal leadership decisions, directly facilitated the flow of funds related to Hamas. Although this is a civil lawsuit rather than a criminal one, it still carries reputational risks and potential financial losses. A civil lawsuit requires proof that Zhao and Binance provided "material assistance" or "knowingly facilitated" harmful activities, even without evidence of their intention to support terrorism. As of now, neither Changpeng Zhao nor Binance has issued a public response. The case is still in its early stages and has been filed in a US federal court, similar to previous legal proceedings. Whether this matter will have a substantial impact on Changpeng Zhao's legal situation remains unclear, but its impact on his personal reputation, the Binance brand, and broader market trust could be significant. Overall, this pattern is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore. Binance and Changpeng Zhao have documented violations and have admitted to these violations in their official plea agreements. Regulators have previously stated that Binance lacked appropriate controls, allowing funds linked to sanctioned organizations and terrorist groups to flow through the platform. Whether these new allegations will be adequately proven in court remains to be seen. The recurring accusations, supported by serious plaintiffs and mainstream media, highlight the lingering shadow of Changpeng Zhao's leadership. This new lawsuit is not isolated. It reflects the increasing global pressure Binance faces, and how Zhao's regulatory misconduct continues to plague him even after his pardon. The unsolved mystery lies not only in whether Zhao facilitated illicit financial flows, but also in whether the entire system he built made such flows predictable, preventable, and ultimately harmful. Currently, the crypto world is once again focused on another high-risk chapter in this protracted drama involving Changpeng Zhao.
